pattern: Committing to kill one enemy fully before moving to the next is always at least as good as interleaving, and an exchange argument on any two adjacent enemies in that order pins down the exact sorting comparator that minimizes total damage.
hints: It is never worse to fully commit to killing one enemy before starting on the next, so the question becomes purely what order to kill them in.

For any two enemies you could swap adjacent in the kill order, compare the extra damage each ordering causes; that comparison gives you a sorting rule based on each enemy's kill time and damage-per-second.

Once sorted, every enemy still alive contributes its damage for the entire time it takes to kill the current one, so sum each enemy's kill time multiplied by the total damage-per-second of everyone from that point onward.

edge_inputs.description: four enemies with distinct damage-to-time ratios
equal damage but increasing health forces an ordering by kill time alone
two enemies at the extreme values of the range
power at its minimum forces long kill times
two enemies tie exactly on the sorting ratio
the highest-damage enemy also has the lowest health, so it must die first

stress.description: random small rosters to exercise the sort comparator
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 8))
    power = rng.randint(1, 50)
    damage = [rng.randint(1, 50) for _ in range(n)]
    health = [rng.randint(1, 50) for _ in range(n)]
    return [power, damage, health]
stress.note: kept small so a permutation cross-check stays feasible during authoring

stress.description: large rosters at the full constraint scale
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = size
    power = rng.randint(1, 10000)
    damage = [rng.randint(1, 10000) for _ in range(n)]
    health = [rng.randint(1, 10000) for _ in range(n)]
    return [power, damage, health]
stress.note: the full 100000-enemy scale, where the total damage can exceed 2^53
